recognized all the world over as a most important

title: Selected Letters of Friedrich Nietzsche (Classic Reprint) - Friedrich Wilhelm Nietzsche: 9781440086137 - AbeBooks
excerpts:
Section Title: ... > Selected Letters of Friedrich Nietzsche (Classic Reprint)
Content:
Paperback. Condition: New. Print on Demand. This book offers a unique glimpse into the private life of one of the most influential thinkers of the 19th century. Through a selection of personal letters, the author reveals a side of the philosopher rarely seen in his published works. We see him not as a detached intellectual, but as a deeply affectionate brother, son, and friend. The letters also shed light on the author's intellectual development, revealing his early engagement with Schopenhauer and his evolving philosophical ideas. The correspondence reveals the author's keen interest in the cultural and political landscape of his time, offering a unique perspective on the rise of German nationalism and the stifling intellectual atmosphere of the period.

title: Friedrich Nietzsche - Simple English Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia
excerpts:
Section Title: Contents
Content:
**Friedrich Nietzsche** (15 October 1844 – 25 August 1900) was a German philosopher and writer . At first, he was a professor at the University of Basel . He worked in a part of classical studies called classical philology . He later wrote several books of philosophy, and is now known as one of the most important thinkers of modern times.

title: Selected Letters of Friedrich Nietzsche - Wikisource, the free online library
excerpts:
Section Title: Selected Letters of Friedrich Nietzsche > To Peter Gast - July, 1883
Content:
[edit ]
```
                                                                                    Sils-Maria, 13 July, 1883.
```
DEAR FRIEND:
Since my last letter I have been better, my spirits
have improved, and all of a sudden I have conceived
the second part of "Thus Spake Zarathustra." The
birth followed upon the conception; the whole thing
was worked out with the greatest vehemence.
(While working upon it, the thought struck me that
one day I should probably die from an outburst and
overflow of emotion like this: the devil take me!)
The MS. will be ready for press the day after tomorrow morning, and my eyes describe limits to my industry.
If you read the last page of *Zarathustra* , Part I, you will find the words:
"—and only when ye have all denied me will I return unto you."
"Verily, with other eyes, my brethren, shall I then
seek my lost ones, with another love shall I then love
you." <su
